heroOverview: Erysipelas is a bacterial skin infection of the upper dermis and superficial lymphatics caused almost exclusively by group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes). It produces a sharply demarcated, raised, fiery-red plaque that advances over hours and is almost always accompanied by fever and systemic upset. Unlike deeper cellulitis, the clear boundary between infected and normal skin is the defining clinical feature. About 1 in 1,000 adults develop erysipelas each year in high-income countries, with recurrence rates reaching 30% over three years when predisposing factors go uncorrected. With prompt antibiotic treatment most episodes resolve within 7–14 days, but the infection can recur and cause lasting lymphatic damage if inadequately managed.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence approximately 1 per 1,000 adults annually in Europe and North America
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in adults over 60; also elevated in infants; affects men and women roughly equally
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal pattern: infants (facial) and adults over 60 (leg)
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated millions of episodes worldwide each year; exact global burden not systematically tracked

definition: Erysipelas (ICD-10: A46) is a non-purulent bacterial infection of the dermis and superficial lymphatics, typically caused by beta-haemolytic Streptococcus, most often group A (S. pyogenes) and occasionally groups B, C, or G. The organism penetrates through a minor break in the skin — a fissure, insect bite, surgical wound, or fungal infection — and spreads through the superficial lymphatic vessels of the dermis. The result is a bright-red, oedematous, warm plaque with a raised, well-defined advancing border that distinguishes erysipelas from the less sharply delineated deeper infection of cellulitis. Systemic signs — fever, rigors, and leucocytosis — typically precede or accompany the rash by hours. Erysipelas falls under infectious disease but is routinely managed by general practitioners, emergency physicians, and dermatologists. The face was the classic site historically, but the lower leg now accounts for 70–80% of cases, largely because venous insufficiency, oedema, and tinea pedis provide the entry portal and tissue environment that support infection.

typesClassification.description: The most common modern form, affecting one lower leg, entering via a fissure or tinea pedis and producing a sharply bordered red plaque with ascending lymphangitis.

More common in the antibiotic era and in infants; typically begins near the nose or ear and can spread across the cheek ('butterfly' distribution); associated with nasopharyngeal streptococcal carriage.
Three or more episodes, usually in the same leg; caused by residual lymphatic damage that perpetuates local oedema and impairs immune surveillance, creating a cycle of repeated infection.

A more severe variant in which tissue pressure and inflammation cause the overlying epidermis to lift, forming blisters; it does not necessarily signal deeper or more dangerous infection but warrants close monitoring.

primarySymptoms: A sharply demarcated, raised, bright-red plaque on the skin that advances over hours — the razor-sharp border is the hallmark that separates erysipelas from deeper cellulitis.
Intense local warmth, swelling, and tenderness over the affected area, driven by the acute inflammatory response in the superficial dermis and lymphatics.
Fever, often 38.5–40°C, frequently preceding visible skin changes by several hours and accompanied by rigors and a feeling of being acutely unwell.
Red streaking (lymphangitis) extending proximally from the plaque along the course of superficial lymphatics, visible as a thin red line tracking up the limb.
Regional lymphadenopathy — tender, enlarged lymph nodes in the groin (for leg erysipelas) or neck (for facial cases) — indicating lymphatic spread of infection.
Peau d'orange appearance of the skin surface within the plaque, caused by lymphatic congestion creating a dimpled texture that resembles orange peel.
Blisters or bullae developing over the plaque in severe or bullous erysipelas as fluid accumulates between the inflamed dermis and overlying epidermis.
Systemic malaise, headache, myalgia, and anorexia accompanying the fever, reflecting circulating streptococcal toxins and the systemic inflammatory response.
In facial erysipelas: swelling and erythema around one or both eyes, sometimes with a classic 'butterfly' distribution spreading from the bridge of the nose.
Pitting oedema persisting in the affected limb for days to weeks after the acute infection resolves, due to lymphatic damage from the infection itself.
earlyWarningSigns: A sudden onset of fever or chills before any skin change is apparent.
A small area of focal warmth, redness, and skin tightness that appears over a few hours rather than a day.
New swelling around a minor skin break such as a toe-web fissure or recent insect bite.
A rapid spread of redness — advancing centimetres within hours is characteristic of erysipelas.
emergencySigns: Purple or black discolouration within the plaque, suggesting necrosis and possible progression to necrotising fasciitis.
Confusion, low blood pressure, rapid heart rate above 100 bpm, or reduced urine output — signs of sepsis requiring emergency resuscitation.
Spreading beyond the plaque into woody-hard, painful deep tissue that does not pit on pressure, raising concern for a deep tissue infection.
High fever above 40°C that does not respond after 24 hours of appropriate antibiotic therapy.
Facial erysipelas with eye pain, visual change, or restricted eye movement, which can indicate cavernous sinus thrombosis.

causes.cause: Group A Streptococcus (S. pyogenes) skin invasion
causes.description: Beta-haemolytic group A streptococci are the primary pathogen in over 80% of cases. The bacterium produces streptokinase, hyaluronidase, and M-protein virulence factors that allow rapid spread through the superficial dermis and lymphatic channels.

Erysipelas almost always enters through a pre-existing break: interdigital tinea pedis fissures, venous ulcers, insect bites, surgical incisions, or excoriated eczema. Intact skin is highly resistant to streptococcal invasion.

Chronic leg oedema from venous disease, lymphoedema, or prior erysipelas provides protein-rich interstitial fluid that supports bacterial growth and impairs the local immune response, creating a permissive environment for infection.
In facial erysipelas, particularly in children, the organism typically originates from streptococcal nasopharyngeal colonisation rather than a skin wound, spreading directly to the facial skin.

causes.cause: Group B, C, or G Streptococcus (minority of cases)
causes.description: Non-group-A streptococci account for roughly 10–15% of erysipelas, more often in elderly patients and those with chronic venous insufficiency; they produce a clinically identical picture but may show different antibiotic susceptibility.

Conditions that impair the adaptive immune response — diabetes, corticosteroid use, lymphoma, or HIV — lower the threshold for streptococcal invasion and predispose to more severe or recurrent episodes.

riskFactors.description: Oedematous limbs have poor lymphatic clearance and protein-rich tissue fluid; each episode of erysipelas damages lymphatics further, doubling the risk of recurrence with each episode.
Toe-web fungal infection creates macerated fissures that are the single most common entry portal for erysipelas of the lower leg; treating tinea pedis reduces recurrence risk by approximately 50%.
Obesity independently predicts erysipelas through increased tissue oedema, impaired lymphatic function, and frequent skin-fold maceration; weight loss reduces leg swelling and recurrence risk.

Peripheral neuropathy reduces detection of minor injuries, microvascular disease impairs healing, and impaired neutrophil function weakens local defence; tight glycaemic control lowers infection frequency.
Insect bites, wounds, surgical incisions, venous ulcers, and excoriated skin conditions all break the skin barrier; prompt wound care and management of skin conditions eliminate common entry points.
Skin thinning, reduced immune function, comorbid venous disease, and reduced mobility all converge in older adults to raise erysipelas incidence about twofold compared with middle-aged adults.
Prior infection leaves lymphatic scarring that perpetuates oedema; at least 30% of patients have a recurrence within three years, and each episode further increases future risk.

Reduced calf muscle pump activity due to wheelchair use, prolonged bed rest, or sedentary lifestyle increases leg swelling and lymphatic stasis; compression therapy and mobility aid substantially reduce risk.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60, who represent the majority of lower-leg cases
Infants and young children, predominantly facial erysipelas
People with chronic venous insufficiency or lymphoedema of any age
Immunocompromised individuals including those receiving systemic corticosteroids or chemotherapy
People with recurrent tinea pedis left untreated
diagnosisOverview: Erysipelas is a clinical diagnosis based on the history and examination. The hallmarks are an abrupt onset of systemic upset — fever, rigors, and malaise — followed within hours by a fiery-red, warm, tender plaque with a sharply raised and well-demarcated border. This sharp border is the key feature distinguishing erysipelas from cellulitis, in which the margin blends gradually into surrounding skin. Blood tests typically show a neutrophil leucocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein, and blood cultures are positive in roughly 2–4% of cases. Swabs of intact erysipelas skin are rarely productive, but any blister or break should be swabbed. Skin punch biopsy reveals dermal oedema, dilated lymphatics, and a dense neutrophilic infiltrate with Gram-positive cocci. The most important differential is non-purulent cellulitis, which has the same systemic picture but lacks the elevated, sharply bordered plaque and classically involves deeper dermis and subcutaneous fat. Necrotising fasciitis must be excluded whenever the patient is severely ill, there is woody-hard induration, skin discolouration, or pain that seems disproportionate to the visible surface changes. Imaging — ultrasound or MRI — is used when necrotising fasciitis cannot be excluded clinically. Venous thrombosis can mimic a first episode of lower-leg erysipelas and, importantly, can coexist with it, so duplex ultrasound is appropriate when there is clinical suspicion. Contact dermatitis, acute gout, and acute lipodermatosclerosis are other mimics to consider.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establish the sharp-bordered, raised erythema, fever onset, and entry portal — together these give a clinical diagnosis with high sensitivity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician inspects the skin and asks about the timeline of redness, fever, any skin breaks, and prior episodes. No painful procedure is needed for the initial diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and C-reactive protein
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the systemic inflammatory response; a neutrophil count above 10×10⁹/L and a CRP above 100 mg/L support a significant bacterial infection and guide admission decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw from the arm; results return within 1–2 hours in a hospital setting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and determines antibiotic sensitivities; positive in about 2–4% of erysipelas but critical when sepsis is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two bottles of blood are drawn aseptically, usually from the arm; results are reported after 24–48 hours incubation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Cultures bacteria directly from a skin break or blister to guide antibiotic choice in cases not responding to empirical treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab is rubbed gently over the entry wound or a blister is punctured and the fluid collected; takes less than a minute and causes minimal discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes deep vein thrombosis, which can present similarly to or coexist with lower-leg erysipelas.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound probe is applied to the leg with gel; the scan takes 15–20 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI or CT of the limb
diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates erysipelas from necrotising fasciitis when clinical findings are ambiguous; MRI shows the depth of tissue involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI takes 30–45 minutes and requires lying still in the scanner; contrast may be injected into a vein. CT is faster and better in an emergency.

treatmentOverview: Penicillin remains the first-line treatment for erysipelas because group A Streptococcus has maintained universal susceptibility to penicillin and resistance has not emerged. For mild-to-moderate cases, oral phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) for 10–14 days is effective in approximately 80–90% of patients. Amoxicillin is an acceptable alternative with better bioavailability. For patients unable to take penicillin due to allergy, cefalexin is a suitable first alternative if the allergy is not anaphylactic; clindamycin or clarithromycin are used when true penicillin hypersensitivity exists. Intravenous benzylpenicillin is reserved for severe cases, rapidly spreading infection, systemic sepsis, immunocompromise, or failure of oral therapy. Most patients are suitable for oral treatment at home, but hospital admission is warranted for fever above 38.5°C persisting after 24 hours of oral antibiotics, haemodynamic instability, rapidly advancing margins, bullous disease, necrotising change, or inability to take oral medication. Elevation of the affected limb substantially reduces oedema and pain. Marking the border of erythema with a skin pen at presentation allows accurate assessment of progression. Antibiotic duration is 10–14 days; shorter courses are associated with higher recurrence rates. For recurrent erysipelas — defined as two or more episodes in 12 months or three or more lifetime episodes — monthly low-dose penicillin prophylaxis for 1–2 years is recommended by European and British guidelines, and treating predisposing factors such as tinea pedis, lymphoedema, and venous insufficiency is essential.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for mild-to-moderate erysipelas; targets group A Streptococcus, which remains universally penicillin-susceptible. Prescribed as 500 mg four times daily for 10–14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in approximately 80–90% of uncomplicated erysipelas episodes when a full 10–14 day course is completed.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred by some guidelines over penicillin V due to superior gastrointestinal absorption; given 500 mg three times daily for 10–14 days and is better tolerated by some patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacteriological and clinical efficacy equivalent to penicillin V; cure rates of approximately 85–90% in observational studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous benzylpenicillin
medicalTreatments.description: For severe, rapidly spreading, or septic erysipelas; achieves high tissue concentrations immediately and is the standard in hospital management, stepped down to oral therapy once the patient improves.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response expected within 24–48 hours in approximately 85–95% of patients; step-down to oral penicillin at 48–72 hours is safe once fever settles and erythema stops advancing.

medicalTreatments.name: Cefalexin
medicalTreatments.description: Oral first-generation cephalosporin used as an alternative when penicillin V is unsuitable (non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy or tolerance issues); 500 mg four times daily for 10–14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates broadly similar to penicillin at approximately 80–85%.

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin
medicalTreatments.description: Used for patients with confirmed penicillin allergy; also covers staphylococcal co-infection if suspected; note regional clindamycin resistance in streptococci exceeds 10% in some areas — local resistance data should inform its use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in approximately 75–85% of streptococcal skin infections, with regional variation driven by resistance rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Long-term penicillin prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Monthly benzathine penicillin intramuscular injection or daily oral penicillin V (250–500 mg twice daily) for 1–2 years in patients with recurrent erysipelas; recommended by CREST, NICE, and European Dermatology Forum guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence rates by approximately 50–70% during the prophylaxis period in randomised trials, though benefit wanes after stopping unless predisposing factors are addressed.

alternativeTreatments.name: Limb elevation
alternativeTreatments.description: Elevating the affected limb above heart level reduces oedema, pain, and the local inflammatory burden; widely recommended as an adjunct to antibiotics and accelerates resolution by 1–2 days in observational data.

alternativeTreatments.name: Compression therapy for lymphoedema
alternativeTreatments.description: Multilayer compression bandaging or graduated compression stockings after the acute episode treats the underlying oedema that predisposes to recurrence; not applied during the acute inflammatory phase.

alternativeTreatments.name: Treatment of tinea pedis
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical or oral antifungals (e.g. terbinafine cream) applied to toe-web fissures eliminate the most common entry portal; a randomised trial showed a 50% reduction in erysipelas recurrence when tinea was eradicated.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist or dermatologist should be involved when erysipelas fails to improve within 48–72 hours of appropriate penicillin, when necrotising fasciitis cannot be excluded, when the patient is severely immunocompromised, when there is a known or suspected resistant organism, or when recurrent erysipelas occurs despite basic preventive measures. A vascular surgeon is appropriate when the underlying driver is severe venous insufficiency or lymphoedema amenable to intervention.
doctorSelectionGuide: For first or straightforward episodes, a general practitioner or emergency physician can initiate treatment. For recurrent erysipelas, seek an infectious disease physician with experience in long-term prophylaxis protocols, or a dermatologist with a skin infection interest. A combined appointment with a lymphoedema nurse specialist or vascular medicine team is valuable when chronic swelling is present. Ask about experience with antibiotic prophylaxis regimens and lymphoedema compression management.
preventionStrategies: Treat tinea pedis promptly and consistently with topical antifungals; eradicating toe-web fissures removes the most common entry portal for erysipelas of the lower leg.
Apply compression stockings or bandaging to chronically oedematous legs to reduce the tissue environment that supports bacterial growth.
Keep the skin well moisturised to prevent drying and fissuring, particularly on the lower legs and feet.
Seek early treatment of any wound, ulcer, or skin break on the leg — especially if there is underlying venous insufficiency.
Maintain tight glycaemic control in diabetes to preserve immune function and skin integrity.
Use long-term penicillin prophylaxis if you have had two or more episodes within 12 months — discuss with your doctor.
lifestyleModifications: Wear well-fitted, supportive footwear and inspect feet daily if you have diabetes or poor circulation.
Maintain a healthy weight to reduce chronic leg oedema and the mechanical pressure on lymphatics.
Exercise regularly — walking activates the calf muscle pump, which is the main driver of venous and lymphatic return from the leg.
Avoid prolonged standing or sitting with dependent legs; take regular breaks to elevate feet.
Keep nails trimmed and skin clean to reduce colonisation with streptococcal organisms.
Do not scratch or pick at skin lesions, eczema, or insect bites on the affected leg.

recommended: Adequate protein to support immune function and skin repair
Plenty of fluids to maintain hydration during fever
Anti-inflammatory foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed) to support tissue healing
High-fibre diet and weight management foods to address obesity-related oedema
avoid: Excess sodium, which worsens fluid retention and leg oedema
Alcohol, which dilates blood vessels and can worsen local oedema
High-sugar foods if you have diabetes, as hyperglycaemia impairs immune defences
Crash diets that deplete protein and micronutrients needed for skin integrity

exerciseGuidelines: During an acute episode, rest the affected limb in an elevated position — horizontal or slightly raised above heart level — to reduce swelling and pain. Avoid weight-bearing on a severely swollen or blistered leg until the acute inflammation settles. Once the fever and advancing margin have resolved, gentle walking is encouraged because it activates the calf muscle pump and promotes lymphatic drainage. After recovery, regular moderate walking, swimming, or cycling is recommended, particularly for patients with chronic leg oedema, to maintain venous and lymphatic function.
dailyManagement: Take the full prescribed antibiotic course even after skin redness begins to fade; stopping early risks relapse.
Photograph the skin border at diagnosis and daily to track whether the erythema is advancing or retreating.
Measure and record your temperature twice a day during treatment; fever persisting beyond 48 hours warrants medical review.
Keep the affected limb elevated when sitting or lying down throughout the acute episode.
Apply prescribed emollient to the skin around the lesion to prevent extension through dry, cracked areas.
For recurrent erysipelas: use your prophylactic antibiotic as prescribed, treat tinea pedis, and wear compression hosiery daily.
prognosis: Most episodes of erysipelas resolve fully with appropriate antibiotic treatment over 7–14 days, and the overall mortality is low — below 1% in otherwise healthy adults. The principal challenge is recurrence: approximately 30% of patients have a further episode within three years when predisposing factors remain unaddressed. Each episode of erysipelas damages superficial lymphatics, raising the threshold for the next episode by increasing baseline oedema, which in turn creates a self-perpetuating cycle. In patients with recurrent disease who receive long-term penicillin prophylaxis and address reversible risk factors, recurrence rates can be reduced by 50–70%. Untreated or inadequately treated erysipelas can progress to bacteraemia, septicaemia, or, rarely, necrotising fasciitis, a life-threatening surgical emergency. Long-term lymphoedema of the affected limb occurs in a minority of patients with multiple recurrences. Facial erysipelas carries a small risk of extension to cavernous sinus thrombosis if the infection involves peri-orbital structures.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically begins to settle within 24–48 hours of starting an effective antibiotic. Skin redness stops advancing within 1–2 days and the plaque begins to soften and fade from the centre outward by day 3–5. Most patients with uncomplicated leg erysipelas can return to normal activity within 7–10 days, though residual skin desquamation, hyperpigmentation, and mild oedema may persist for 2–4 weeks. Bullous erysipelas or cases requiring hospitalisation may take 2–3 weeks to resolve fully. Any swelling persisting beyond four weeks should prompt assessment for chronic lymphoedema.
complications: Recurrent erysipelas — each episode damages lymphatics and raises the risk of further infection, with 30% of patients experiencing recurrence within three years.
Chronic lymphoedema — persistent, non-pitting oedema of the limb resulting from cumulative lymphatic scarring over multiple episodes.
Bacteraemia and sepsis — rare but life-threatening spread of streptococci into the bloodstream, particularly in elderly or immunocompromised patients.

Necrotising fasciitis — very rare but critical complication involving deep fascial plane infection, which may begin as or be misdiagnosed as erysipelas; carries significant mortality without urgent surgery.
Cavernous sinus thrombosis — a rare complication of facial erysipelas involving thrombotic extension into the venous sinuses of the brain, presenting with eye pain, proptosis, and meningism.

Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis — immune-mediated kidney inflammation following group A streptococcal infection, presenting with haematuria, proteinuria, and hypertension weeks after the acute episode.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cellulitis (includes erysipelas)
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS guidance covering symptoms, treatment, and when to seek urgent care for skin bacterial infections including erysipelas.

supportResources.name: NICE — Cellulitis and Erysipelas: Antimicrobial Prescribing (NG141)
supportResources.description: UK national guideline for antibiotic selection and duration in cellulitis and erysipelas, including when to use prophylaxis for recurrent disease.

supportResources.name: CDC — Group A Streptococcal Infections
supportResources.description: Factual overview of group A streptococcal infections — the pathogen responsible for most erysipelas — from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

sources.title: NICE NG141 — Cellulitis and Erysipelas: Antimicrobial Prescribing (2019)
Stevens DL et al. Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections: IDSA 2014 Update
Picard D et al. Risk factors for erysipelas of the leg: case-control study. BMJ, 1999
Thomas K et al. Prophylactic antibiotics for the prevention of cellulitis (erysipelas) of the leg. Cochrane Database Systematic Reviews, 2019

confusedWithConditions.name: Cellulitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cellulitis is a deeper infection of the dermis and subcutaneous fat with diffuse, poorly demarcated margins, whereas erysipelas has a sharply raised and clearly defined border involving only the upper dermis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Necrotising fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Necrotising fasciitis involves deep fascial planes with woody-hard induration, grey or purple skin discolouration, and severe pain disproportionate to surface changes — distinguishing it from the sharply bordered surface plaque of erysipelas.

confusedWithConditions.name: Impetigo
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Impetigo is a superficial crusting infection without systemic fever or the raised advancing border of erysipelas, typically affecting the face in children.

relatedConditions.name: Cellulitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most closely related condition — also a bacterial skin infection, distinguished by deeper tissue involvement and a less defined margin.

relatedConditions.name: Necrotising fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most dangerous differential, requiring immediate surgical assessment; distinguishing necrotising fasciitis from erysipelas is critical.

relatedConditions.name: Impetigo
relatedConditions.relevance: Another superficial streptococcal or staphylococcal skin infection that shares the same pathogen and entry-portal mechanism.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphangitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Lymphangitis — red streaking tracking from the plaque — commonly accompanies erysipelas and signals spread along superficial lymphatics.

faqs.question: What makes erysipelas different from cellulitis?
faqs.answer: The key difference is the border: erysipelas has a sharply raised, clearly defined edge where infected skin meets normal skin, while cellulitis blends gradually into surrounding tissue. Erysipelas also involves only the upper dermis and lymphatics, whereas cellulitis goes deeper into subcutaneous fat.

faqs.question: What causes erysipelas?
faqs.answer: Erysipelas is caused mainly by group A Streptococcus bacteria entering through a break in the skin — most often a fissure from athlete's foot, an insect bite, or a minor wound. The bacteria spread rapidly through the superficial skin lymphatics, triggering an intense inflammatory response.

faqs.question: Is erysipelas contagious?
faqs.answer: Erysipelas is not meaningfully contagious through casual contact. The bacterium comes from the patient's own skin flora or bowel; transmission to another healthy person requires direct inoculation through broken skin, which rarely occurs in normal daily contact.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats erysipelas?
faqs.answer: Penicillin is the first-line treatment because group A Streptococcus remains universally sensitive to it. Oral penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10–14 days works for mild cases; severe or spreading erysipelas requires intravenous benzylpenicillin in hospital. Clindamycin or cefalexin are used when penicillin allergy is confirmed.

faqs.question: How long does erysipelas take to heal?
faqs.answer: Fever usually settles within 24–48 hours of starting effective antibiotics, and the red plaque stops advancing within 1–2 days. Most uncomplicated cases clear within 7–14 days, though skin peeling and mild swelling can linger for 2–4 weeks. The full antibiotic course should always be completed.

faqs.question: Can erysipelas come back?
faqs.answer: Yes — about 30% of patients have a recurrence within three years, usually because predisposing factors such as lymphoedema, athlete's foot, or chronic leg swelling remain untreated. Each episode damages lymphatics and raises the risk of another. Long-term low-dose penicillin prophylaxis can reduce recurrence by 50–70% when predisposing factors are also addressed.

faqs.question: When should I go to hospital for erysipelas?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care or same-day hospital review if you have a very high fever not settling after 24 hours of oral antibiotics, rapid spread of the redness, purple or black areas in the plaque, confusion, dizziness, or a fast pulse. Facial erysipelas near the eye with visual changes also requires urgent assessment.

faqs.question: Can erysipelas affect the face?
faqs.answer: Yes. Facial erysipelas, once the most common form, typically starts near the nose or ear and spreads across the cheek. It is more common in infants and in adults with nasal streptococcal carriage. Facial cases near the eye carry a small risk of spreading to the cavernous sinus and warrant prompt treatment.

faqs.question: Does erysipelas need to be treated in hospital?
faqs.answer: Most cases can be managed at home with oral antibiotics, rest, and limb elevation. Hospital admission is needed when fever persists beyond 24 hours on oral antibiotics, when the redness is rapidly spreading, when the patient is systemically unwell, immunocompromised, or unable to take oral medication, or when necrotising fasciitis cannot be excluded.

faqs.question: How do I prevent erysipelas from coming back?
faqs.answer: Treat athlete's foot promptly and keep it clear, as toe-web fissures are the most common entry point. Use compression stockings if you have chronic leg swelling. Maintain a healthy weight, moisturise dry skin, and seek early care for any wound on the leg. If you have had two or more episodes in a year, ask about long-dose penicillin prophylaxis.

faqs.question: Can erysipelas cause permanent swelling in the leg?
faqs.answer: Repeated episodes can scar the superficial lymphatics and cause a degree of chronic lymphoedema — persistent, non-pitting swelling that outlasts each individual infection. The risk rises with each recurrence. Treating predisposing factors and using compression therapy after episodes helps prevent this from becoming permanent.

faqs.question: Is the skin discolouration from erysipelas permanent?
faqs.answer: The redness, heat, and swelling resolve once the infection clears, but temporary skin darkening (post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation) and superficial peeling are common in the weeks after. These usually fade within 1–3 months. Persistent dark discolouration may reflect underlying venous insufficiency rather than the infection itself.

faqs.question: What is bullous erysipelas?
faqs.answer: Bullous erysipelas is a variant in which fluid accumulates between the inflamed dermis and the overlying epidermis, forming visible blisters or bullae on the surface of the plaque. It does not necessarily mean a deeper or more dangerous infection but warrants hospital assessment to confirm the diagnosis and ensure the infection is not progressing.

faqs.question: Can erysipelas lead to sepsis?
faqs.answer: Erysipelas can spread to the bloodstream in a small minority of cases, particularly in elderly or immunocompromised patients. Blood cultures are positive in roughly 2–4% of hospital cases. Early and complete antibiotic treatment dramatically reduces this risk, which is why starting therapy promptly matters.

faqs.question: Is erysipelas the same as St Anthony's Fire?
faqs.answer: St Anthony's Fire is a historical term applied to erysipelas because of the vivid, flame-like redness and burning sensation of the infection. The term was also used for ergotism (a toxin from fungal grain contamination) in the Middle Ages. Today it specifically refers to erysipelas in most medical contexts.

faqs.question: Does erysipelas always need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Yes. Unlike some very mild superficial skin conditions, erysipelas is a bacterial infection with a systemic inflammatory response that requires antibiotics. Penicillin is the first-line choice and group A Streptococcus has not developed resistance to it, so treatment is reliably effective when started promptly.

faqs.question: Can children get erysipelas?
faqs.answer: Yes. In infants and young children erysipelas most often affects the face, typically spreading from near the nose where streptococcal nasopharyngeal carriage is common. Childhood erysipelas tends to respond well to penicillin; prompt treatment is important to prevent spread to the peri-orbital tissues.

faqs.question: What is the role of elevation in erysipelas treatment?
faqs.answer: Elevating the affected limb above the level of the heart reduces oedema and pain by improving venous and lymphatic drainage from the inflamed tissue. It is recommended as a routine adjunct to antibiotics and may accelerate clinical improvement by 1–2 days. Elevation should be maintained throughout the acute phase.

faqs.question: How is erysipelas diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Erysipelas is diagnosed clinically from the characteristic raised, sharply bordered red plaque with abrupt fever. Blood tests show raised white cell count and CRP. Blood cultures are taken but are positive in only 2–4% of cases. When there is any doubt about necrotising fasciitis, ultrasound or MRI can show the depth of tissue involvement.

faqs.question: Can erysipelas occur on the arm?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is less common than lower-leg or facial involvement. Arm erysipelas can follow a wound, insect bite, or lymphoedema after breast cancer surgery where axillary lymph nodes have been removed. The clinical features and treatment are the same as for leg erysipelas.

metaDescription: Erysipelas is a bacterial skin infection causing a sharply bordered red plaque with fever. Learn symptoms, penicillin treatment and recurrence prevention.
